summary: Spolsky mocks a Microsoft job posting filled with incomprehensible internal jargon and acronyms, arguing it reveals how insular the company has become. He notes the irony that Microsoft needs a team to compete against open-source alternatives, yet can't even communicate clearly with the outside world. He draws a parallel to IBM's notorious habit of using proprietary terminology for common concepts, which Microsoft itself used to ridicule. The core problem isn't that technical people speak in jargon—it's that even Microsoft's marketing people have become unintelligible and don't realize it.
key_points: Microsoft's job postings are so laden with internal acronyms (KT, WHI, v-team, Steve B) that outsiders cannot parse them
The insularity of a 93,000-person company creates a self-reinforcing bubble where nobody notices they've stopped communicating clearly
Microsoft has become the very thing it once mocked IBM for—an organization with its own incomprehensible private vocabulary
The real danger isn't technical jargon among engineers, but marketing people who don't realize they're speaking a language only insiders understand
money_quotes: The whole reason Microsoft even needs a v-team of 13, um, "V DASHES" to compete against Open Office is that they've become so insular that their job postings are full of incomprehensible jargon and acronyms which nobody outside the company can understand.

With 93,000 employees, nobody ever talks to anyone outside the company, so it's no surprise they've become a bizarre borg of "KT", "Steve B", "v-team", "high WHI," CSI, GM, BG, BMO (bowel movements?) and whatnot.
When I worked at Microsoft almost two decades ago we made fun of IBM for having a different word for everything.
Everybody said, "Hard Drive," IBM said "Fixed Disk." Everybody said, "PC," IBM said "Workstation."
But at Microsoft you can't even understand the marketing people, and, what's worse, they don't seem to know that they're speaking in their own special language, understood only to them.
key_insight: When a company grows so large and insular that even its marketing people communicate in impenetrable internal jargon without realizing it, the organization has lost the ability to connect with the outside world.
